findings.description: Page 1 PDF is a dense, wide multi-column table with many columns spanning the full width. The HTML conversion renders this as a much more vertically expanded layout with significantly different spatial arrangement, though the data appears preserved.
findings.evidence: PDF shows a very compact, horizontally dense table with ~20+ columns fitting on one page. HTML renders the same table but with much more vertical spacing and the columns appear stretched out differently.

findings.description: The main table on page 1 has complex multi-level headers with merged cells spanning multiple columns. The HTML version appears to have simplified the header structure, potentially losing some of the hierarchical grouping of column headers.
findings.evidence: PDF shows grouped headers like 'Standard Deduction+:', 'Personal Exemption Allowance++:', 'Computed NC Taxable Income [includes returns with deficit]' spanning multiple sub-columns. HTML rendering shows these headers but the visual grouping/spanning appears different.

findings.description: Page 2 in the PDF contains two distinct table sections (Section A: BY SIZE OF NC TAXABLE INCOME and Section B: BY SIZE OF FEDERAL ADJUSTED GROSS INCOME) on a single page. The HTML separates these into a much more vertically expanded layout.
findings.evidence: PDF has both tables compactly on one page. HTML stretches them out significantly with more vertical space between rows.

findings.description: Income level labels in the first column show slight formatting differences between PDF and HTML versions.
findings.evidence: PDF shows ranges like '$1 - 2,000' in compact form; HTML shows them similarly but with different spacing/alignment.

findings.description: The footnotes at the bottom of both pages have slightly different text formatting and density in the HTML compared to the PDF.
findings.evidence: PDF footnotes are very compact small text. HTML footnotes are rendered with more spacing and potentially different font sizing.

findings.description: The FAGI Level section (Section B) table in page 2 has the same structure but the row for 'Non-Positive AGI' and income brackets appear with slightly different alignment in HTML vs PDF.
findings.evidence: PDF shows tightly packed rows; HTML shows the same data with more generous row spacing.

findings.description: Reading order is preserved - both pages maintain the same top-to-bottom sequence of title, table headers, data rows, totals, and footnotes.
findings.evidence: Content sequence matches between PDF and HTML on both pages.

findings.description: The TOTAL row in Section B (FAGI Level) has a different Effective Tax Rate in the HTML (3.35%) compared to the PDF (5.94%). The PDF shows the same total effective tax rate of 5.94% for both Section A and Section B totals.
findings.evidence: PDF Section B TOTAL row final column: 5.94%. HTML Section B TOTAL row final column: 3.35%.

findings.description: The TOTAL row in Section A has an Effective Tax Rate of 5.94% in the HTML, but the PDF shows two rate columns - one for NCTI basis and one for FAGI basis. The PDF's NCTI-basis rate appears to be 6.96% (not shown distinctly for total) and the FAGI-basis rate is 5.94%. The HTML only shows one effective tax rate column, potentially conflating the two different rate measures.
findings.evidence: PDF has two effective tax rate columns (NCTI basis and FAGI basis). HTML has only one 'Effective Tax Rate*' column.

findings.description: Several numeric values in the HTML for 'No Taxable Income' row appear truncated. The PDF shows the 'before residency proration' value as (3,323,392,356) but the original table text shows '(3,323, - 392,356)' suggesting a formatting artifact. Also the 'after residency proration' column in the PDF for No Taxable Income appears to show (3,905,564,101) which matches the HTML.
findings.evidence: PDF No Taxable Income row: Computed NC Taxable Income before proration shows formatting artifact. HTML shows (3,323,392,356).

findings.description: The HTML has a nested <main> element inside another <main> element, which is invalid HTML structure, though not a heading hierarchy issue per se.
findings.evidence: HTML contains <main> wrapper with another <main> inside it.

findings.description: The PDF contains two distinct effective tax rate columns - one for NCTI basis and one for FAGI basis - but the HTML only includes a single 'Effective Tax Rate*' column, losing an entire column of data from the table.
findings.evidence: PDF column headers show both 'Effective Tax Rate*' for NCTI basis and 'Effective Tax Rate' for FAGI basis. The HTML header only has 'Effective Tax Rate* [%]' once. For example, the $200,001 or more row in Section A shows 6.84% in the HTML but the PDF shows both 6.84% (NCTI) and 6.96% or similar values for the two columns.

findings.description: In Section B TOTAL row, the Effective Tax Rate is shown as 3.35% in the HTML. The PDF shows 5.94% for the FAGI-basis total. This is a substantive numeric error that changes the meaning of the data.
findings.evidence: PDF TOTAL row Section B: 5.94%. HTML TOTAL row Section B: 3.35%.

findings.description: Multiple rows in the PDF's Section A contain two effective tax rate values (NCTI rate and FAGI rate columns), but the HTML only captures one. For example, for the '$200,001 or more' row, the PDF shows effective rates around 6.84% and 6.96% in two separate columns, but the HTML only shows 6.84%.
findings.evidence: PDF has dual rate columns for every income level row. HTML has single rate column.
